text: Both eccentric training and heavy slow resistance training produce significant improvements in Achilles tendinopathy symptoms and function over 12 weeks, with improvements maintained at 52-week follow-up.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Heavy slow resistance training and eccentric training produce equally good clinical outcomes for midportion Achilles tendinopathy with no significant difference between groups.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Training session compliance is significantly higher with heavy slow resistance training (92%) compared to eccentric training (78%) for Achilles tendinopathy rehabilitation.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Patient satisfaction after 12 weeks of Achilles tendinopathy treatment tends to be higher with heavy slow resistance training (100%) than eccentric training (80%), though the difference is not statistically significant.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Both eccentric and heavy slow resistance training for Achilles tendinopathy produce significant reductions in tendon thickness and neovascularization alongside clinical improvement.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Achilles tendinopathy symptoms lasting longer than 3 months (chronic) respond well to structured loading-based treatment programs of 12 weeks duration.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy
